topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a soft, undeniable thud. My thumb hovered over the 'read' button, then back over it, a slow, deliberate hesitation. *Cancelled.* The single word hung in the digital void, heavy and strangely hollow. I reread the message three times, my eyes tracing the familiar font, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that would somehow invalidate the entire communication. My shoulders slumped almost imperceptibly, the tension that had been coiled tight in my chest suddenly unraveling into a dull ache. I stood up, pacing the length of my living room, the carpet fibers feeling strangely alien beneath my bare feet. Who would cancel a party? It felt like a deliberate erasure of something significant. A strange, tight knot formed just beneath my sternum, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I walked to the window, looking out at the ordinary street, suddenly seeing everything as muted, lacking the vibrant color it usually possessed.

[story 2]

I stared at the text message, my jaw slightly slack. It was a stark notification, devoid of the usual celebratory emojis that usually accompanied such news. My fingers trembled slightly as I tapped the screen, the action feeling clumsy and exaggerated. This wasn't right. The planning had been meticulous; the decorations were already being sourced, the catering confirmed. To find this notification—this abrupt cessation of everything—felt like a physical jolt. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, shaky exhale. My gaze drifted around the room, cataloging the remnants of anticipation—a half-inflated balloon, a forgotten string of fairy lights. A low murmur escaped my throat, a sound more like a questioning sigh than an actual word. I picked up my phone again, my thumb tracing the screen, unable to settle on what to do next.

[story 3]

When Sarah’s message appeared, I froze mid-sip of my lukewarm coffee. The steam rising from the mug seemed to mock the sudden stillness of my hands. I set the cup down on the coaster with excessive care, the small clink echoing loudly in the quiet apartment. I reread the notification, my eyes flickering back and forth across the screen, trying to reconcile the text with the vibrant mental image I had built of the evening. A strange lightness settled over me, quickly replaced by a heavy, inert feeling in my limbs. I began to tap a rhythm on the table with my index finger, a repetitive, anxious motion. *No. This can’t be right.* I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing to check the door, needing some tangible proof that the reality I was experiencing wasn't dissolving around the edges. The air in the room felt suddenly thin.
